items.title: Python package and module layout
items.description: Use a src-layout (project code under src/<package>/) so tests run against the installed package, not the working directory, surfacing missing-import and packaging bugs early. Keep modules cohesive and shallow: group by domain (ingestion, retrieval, serving) rather than by technical layer alone, and expose a stable public surface via __init__.py while keeping internals private (leading underscore). Declare dependencies and metadata in pyproject.toml (PEP 621) with pinned ranges, and isolate environments per project with a virtualenv or uv/venv lock file.
items.applicability: Applies to any Python service, library, or AI/RAG component that will be tested, packaged, or imported by other code.
items.example: src/myrag/__init__.py exports `from myrag.retrieval import retrieve`; tests/ live at repo root; `pyproject.toml` lists `dependencies = ["fastapi>=0.110,<1.0"]`; build via `pip install -e .` so `import myrag` works identically in tests and prod.
items.limitation: Src-layout requires an editable install or correct PYTHONPATH; running scripts directly from inside the package can mask import errors that only appear when installed.

items.title: FastAPI-style service architecture
items.description: Structure an ASGI service in layers: thin routers (request/response and validation only), a service/use-case layer (business logic, no framework types), and adapters (DB, vector store, model client). Use Pydantic models for request/response schemas so validation and OpenAPI docs are automatic, and inject collaborators via FastAPI dependencies (Depends) so they can be swapped or mocked. Treat external AI/vector services as optional dependencies that degrade gracefully when unreachable rather than letting an endpoint hang on them.
items.applicability: Applies when building Python HTTP/JSON services, especially RAG or model-serving endpoints that must stay responsive when Qdrant/Ollama are down.
items.example: @app.post('/search') async def search(q: SearchRequest, store: VectorStore = Depends(get_store)) -> SearchResponse: results = await service.search(q.text); the router never touches Qdrant directly. Set explicit timeouts on outbound calls (httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=2.0)) so a slow model never blocks the request thread.
items.limitation: Async correctness requires non-blocking I/O end-to-end; a single synchronous/CPU-bound call (e.g. local embedding without an executor) blocks the event loop and stalls all concurrent requests.

items.title: Background workers and task queues
items.description: Move slow or bursty work (ingestion, embedding, batch inference) off the request path into a background worker so HTTP endpoints return immediately. A durable queue (a DB-backed table, Celery/RQ, or a hosted service draining a job table) decouples producers from consumers and survives restarts. Make jobs idempotent and retryable with backoff, record per-job status, and bound concurrency so a flood of imports cannot exhaust the database or model server.
items.applicability: Applies to ingestion pipelines, embedding/index builds, and any operation too slow or unreliable to run inline during a request.
items.example: An endpoint inserts an ImportJob row (status=Queued) and returns 202; a hosted background service polls the table, claims one job (status=Running) under a row lock, processes it, and sets Completed/Failed with an error message. Retries use exponential backoff and a max-attempts cap to avoid poison-message loops.
items.limitation: A DB-backed queue is simple and survives restarts but does not scale to very high throughput and needs careful locking to avoid double-processing; in-memory queues lose work on crash.

items.title: Data pipeline stages
items.description: Model ingestion as explicit, ordered stages: extract (read raw source), validate (schema, encoding, size, content-type), transform/normalize (clean text, chunk, enrich metadata), load (persist canonical rows), and index (optional embeddings/vectors). Persist the canonical result in the primary store before any optional indexing so the pipeline produces durable value even when the vector store is absent. Make each stage observable (counts in/out, skip reasons) and resumable so a failure mid-pipeline does not require reprocessing everything.
items.applicability: Applies to importing repositories, documents, or datasets where a subset of records may be malformed and downstream AI indexing is optional.
items.example: Import a ZIP: extract files, skip binaries and oversized blobs with a recorded reason, decode text honestly (detect encoding, never crash on bad bytes), store ImportedFile rows in MSSQL, then queue embedding as a separate optional stage. Gap reporting surfaces every skipped file so coverage is never silently incomplete.
items.limitation: Strict per-stage validation can reject borderline-valid data; treat skips as reportable gaps, not silent drops, or you lose visibility into what was never ingested.

items.title: Qdrant and vector store basics
items.description: A vector store (e.g. Qdrant) holds high-dimensional embedding vectors and serves approximate-nearest-neighbour (ANN) search by a distance metric (cosine, dot, or L2) over an index such as HNSW. Each point carries an id, a vector of fixed dimension, and a payload (metadata) used for filtering and to map results back to source records. Collections must be created with a dimension and metric that exactly match the embedding model; mismatches produce errors or silently meaningless results.
items.applicability: Applies when storing and querying embeddings for semantic search or RAG retrieval; relevant whenever similarity rather than exact match is needed.
items.example: Create a collection with size=768, distance=Cosine for nomic-embed-text; upsert points {id, vector, payload:{projectId, chunkId, sourcePath}}; query top_k=8 with a payload filter projectId==42. projectId=0 can denote global/shared knowledge.
items.limitation: ANN search is approximate (recall < 100%) and tuned by index parameters; results are only as good as the embeddings, and the store is optional infrastructure that must not be a hard dependency of the primary system.

items.title: Vector store vs keyword search
items.description: Keyword/lexical search (BM25, SQL LIKE/full-text) matches exact tokens and excels at identifiers, error codes, rare terms, and precise recall; vector search matches semantic similarity and excels at paraphrase, synonyms, and conceptual queries. They fail differently: lexical misses synonyms and reworded questions, while vector search can return topically-near but factually-wrong passages and struggles with exact tokens like a specific account number or symbol name. Hybrid retrieval (run both, then fuse/rerank, e.g. reciprocal rank fusion) usually beats either alone.
items.applicability: Applies when choosing a retrieval strategy for code, documents, or mixed corpora where queries range from exact symbols to natural-language intent.
items.example: Query 'why does ProcessCheque time out' benefits from vector recall of related logic; query 'TransactionId 884213' or 'ETAMS' benefits from exact keyword match. A hybrid retriever runs full-text in MSSQL and ANN in Qdrant, then fuses ranks — and still works lexically when Qdrant is offline.
items.limitation: Hybrid adds latency and ranking complexity; without keyword fallback, a system that depends solely on vectors becomes unusable whenever the vector store is unavailable.

items.title: Embeddings: when they help and when they do not
items.description: Embeddings map text to vectors so semantically similar text lands nearby, enabling fuzzy semantic retrieval, clustering, and dedup. They help most when queries are paraphrased natural language over prose-like content and when the embedding model's domain matches the corpus. They help little — and can mislead — for exact-token lookups, very short or code-heavy snippets, highly numeric data, or domains far from the model's training distribution, where cosine similarity does not track real relevance.
items.applicability: Applies when deciding whether to embed a given corpus/field and how to chunk it before indexing.
items.example: Embedding banking-domain prose for 'how is a returned cheque reconciled' works well; embedding raw stack traces, GUIDs, or column-name lists adds cost with poor relevance. Chunk on semantic boundaries (functions, sections) with small overlap; tag each chunk with metadata for filtering.
items.limitation: Embeddings are model- and version-specific: re-embedding with a different or upgraded model invalidates the whole index, and the same dimension/metric must be used consistently or similarity scores become meaningless.

items.title: RAG basics and failure modes
items.description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation retrieves relevant context (from keyword and/or vector search) and injects it into the model prompt so answers are grounded in your data rather than the model's parametric memory. Quality is dominated by retrieval: if the right passage is not retrieved, the model cannot use it, and if irrelevant passages dominate the context, they distract or mislead. Curate and rank what enters the prompt, attribute every claim to a retrieved source, and prefer approved/authoritative knowledge first.
items.applicability: Applies to any system that answers questions over a private corpus, including code/knowledge assistants over imported projects.
items.example: Pipeline: embed/store chunks, retrieve top_k with hybrid search, rerank, place curated approved snippets first in the prompt, then generate with explicit instruction to cite chunk ids and to say 'not found in sources' when context is insufficient.
items.limitation: Named failure modes: a stale index (sources changed but embeddings were not rebuilt), chunk-boundary loss (the answer spans two chunks and neither is fully retrieved), hallucinated citations (the model invents a source id), and context dilution (too many low-relevance chunks crowd out the right one). RAG reduces but never eliminates hallucination.

items.title: Local and offline AI constraints
items.description: A local-first AI platform must assume no GPU, no internet, and no guaranteed model/vector services — the primary relational store (MSSQL) must function standalone and every page or core flow must render without calling an external model or vector DB synchronously. Treat Ollama and Qdrant as optional, config-gated capabilities probed by a background health check and read from a cached snapshot, never blocking the request path. Local CPU inference is slow and memory-bound, so size models to the hardware, cache aggressively, and degrade to non-AI behaviour rather than failing.
items.applicability: Applies to architecture and runtime decisions for air-gapped or hardware-constrained deployments where AI is an enhancement, not a prerequisite.
items.example: Health monitor probes Qdrant/Ollama on a timer and caches status; controllers read IServiceHealthCache, never the live service. With Ollama absent, search falls back to keyword retrieval and the UI shows 'AI features unavailable' instead of hanging. Default local models: a coder LLM plus a 768-dim embedder.
items.limitation: Local models are smaller and weaker than hosted frontier models — lower reasoning quality, smaller context windows, and slower throughput — and offline operation forecloses live web/tool augmentation, so accuracy on novel or current information is limited.

items.title: Prompt and version control hygiene
items.description: Treat prompts, system messages, and task profiles as versioned artifacts under source control, not inline string literals scattered through code. Pin the model id, prompt template version, and key parameters (temperature, max tokens) together so a given output can be reproduced and a regression can be bisected to a specific change. Keep secrets out of prompts and repos, and separate the stable instruction template from per-request variable data so changes are reviewable and diffable.
items.applicability: Applies wherever prompts drive model behaviour and reproducibility, auditability, or A/B comparison matters.
items.example: Store prompt templates as versioned files/records (prompt v1.3, model qwen-coder, temp 0.2); log {promptVersion, modelId, params, retrievedChunkIds} with each generation so an answer is traceable. Inject curated approved knowledge first, then the user query, never hard-coding credentials or PII into the template.
items.limitation: Versioning the prompt does not version the model weights or the retrieved corpus; identical prompts can yield different outputs after a model upgrade or index change, so capture those references too.

items.title: Model limitation disclosure
items.description: Be explicit, in UI and outputs, about what the model can and cannot do: it can produce fluent, plausible text that is confidently wrong (hallucination), it has a training-data cutoff and no inherent knowledge of your private or current data except what is retrieved, and local models are weaker than hosted ones. Surface provenance — which sources grounded an answer and which parts are model inference — and make uncertainty visible rather than presenting all output as authoritative. For a banking/middleware estate, this disclosure is a safety and trust requirement, not a nicety.
items.applicability: Applies to any user-facing AI output, especially where decisions, code changes, or business rules depend on the answer.
items.example: An answer shows cited chunk ids with a confidence indicator and a banner: 'Generated by a local model from retrieved sources; verify against the cited code before acting.' When retrieval is empty, the system states 'no supporting source found' instead of fabricating one.
items.limitation: Disclosure mitigates but does not remove risk — users may over-trust fluent output regardless of warnings, and confidence scores are themselves estimates that can be miscalibrated.

items.title: Evaluation patterns for AI outputs
items.description: Evaluate AI systems on a curated, version-controlled set of representative cases with known-good expectations, measuring retrieval and generation separately. For retrieval, track recall@k and whether the gold passage is present; for generation, track groundedness (claims supported by retrieved context), correctness, and citation validity. Combine cheap automatic checks (exact/regex/structured asserts, retrieval hit rate) with sampled human or LLM-as-judge review, and re-run the suite on every model, prompt, or index change to catch regressions.
items.applicability: Applies whenever AI behaviour must be validated before release or compared across model/prompt/index versions.
items.example: A golden set of 50 domain questions records expected source chunks and key facts; CI computes recall@8 and a groundedness score, flags any answer whose citations are not in the retrieved set, and blocks a prompt change that drops recall below threshold. New models are scored on the same suite before adoption.
items.limitation: LLM-as-judge graders are themselves fallible and can be biased or gamed, automatic metrics miss nuance, and a small golden set may not represent production distribution — treat evaluation as a guardrail, not proof of correctness.
